subject: History
stem: Consider the following statements about Stone Age in India:
subs: 1. Different periods are identified on the basis of the type and technology of stone tools.
2. There are no regional variations in the type and technology of tools in different periods.
3. Stone Age cultures of different periods evolved uniformly in a neat unilinear fashion all over the subcontinent.
explanation: Archaeologists divide the Stone Age (Palaeolithic → Mesolithic → Neolithic) precisely by tool technology, so statement 1 is correct. Statements 2 and 3 are wrong: tools and cultures show strong regional variation and did not develop in one uniform, straight-line sequence everywhere — different regions advanced at different paces.

subject: History
stem: Where is the Vikramkhol Cave located?
explanation: Vikramkhol (Bikramkhol), famous for its prehistoric rock inscriptions/engravings, is in the Jharsuguda district of Odisha. It is not in Bihar, Telangana or West Bengal.

subject: History
stem: Consider the following statements regarding the Levallois Technique:
subs: 1. It refers to making perforated Harappa pottery.
2. It refers to making prehistoric flake tools.
explanation: The Levallois technique is a prehistoric stone-knapping method in which a core is carefully prepared so that flakes of predetermined shape can be struck off — so statement 2 is correct. It has nothing to do with Harappan pottery, so statement 1 is wrong.

subject: History
stem: With reference to the Mesolithic art in India, consider the following statements:
subs: 1. Alexander Burns discovered the first rock paintings in India at Sohagighat in Uttar Pradesh.
2. Bhimbetka rock paintings were discovered when archaeologist V.S. Wakankar noticed the rocks out of a train window while travelling from Bhopal to Itarsi.
3. The paintings and engravings do not exist in the same shelter in the rock art of Orissa.
explanation: The famous story that archaeologist V.S. Wakankar spotted the Bhimbetka rock shelters from a train is correct (2). Statement 1 misnames the discoverer (the first Indian rock paintings were reported by Archibald Carlleyle, not "Alexander Burns"), and statement 3 about Orissa rock art is inaccurate. Hence 2 only.

subject: History
stem: Consider the following statements about the Jorwe culture of the Deccan:
subs: 1. It covers practically the whole of modern Maharashtra except the coastal Konkan district.
2. The Pravara-Godavari valleys seem to have been the nuclear zone.
3. The large sites of this culture yield evidence of shifting agriculture.
explanation: The Jorwe (a Chalcolithic culture, c. 1400–700 BCE) spread across Maharashtra except Konkan (1), centred on the Pravara-Godavari valleys (2). Statement 3 is wrong — Jorwe people practised settled farming (wheat, barley, lentils) with irrigation, not shifting cultivation. Hence 1 and 2 only.

subject: History
stem: Recently, archaeological studies at which one among the following revealed that by 1200 BCE there was a flourishing agrarian civilization and the cultivation of rice was well-established at this place?
explanation: Excavations at Sivakalai/Adichanallur on the Thamirabarani (Tamil Nadu) dated rice and an agrarian settlement to about 1200 BCE, pushing back the antiquity of Tamil civilisation. The other sites are not linked to this finding.

subject: History
stem: Consider the following statements about Harappan cities:
subs: 1. Roads were not always absolutely straight and did not always cross one another at right angles.
2. A striking feature is the uniformity in the average size of the bricks for houses and for city walls. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: Harappan town planning used a grid, but in practice roads were not always dead-straight (1). A hallmark of the civilization was its standardised baked bricks in a fixed 1:2:4 ratio, used uniformly across cities (2). Both statements are correct.

subject: History
stem: Which one of the following statements about the Harappan Culture is not correct?
options: It witnessed the first cities in the subcontinent.
It marks the first use of script, written from right to left.
It marks the earliest known use of iron as a medium for the art of sculpting.
It marks the earliest known use of stone as a medium for the art of sculpting.
explanation: The Harappan was a Bronze Age civilization — it did not use iron at all (iron came much later, in the Vedic age). So "earliest use of iron" is false. It did have the first cities, an undeciphered right-to-left script, and early stone/bronze sculpture.

subject: History
stem: Match List-I with List-II and select the correct answer using the code given below the Lists: List-I (IVC Site) List-II (Location) A. Dholavira - 1. Saurashtra B. Rakhigarhi - 2. Hisar C. Bhirrana - 3. Kadir Island D. Bhogavo - 4. Haryana Code: A B C D
explanation: Dholavira stands on Kadir (Khadir) Island in the Rann of Kutch (3); Rakhigarhi is in Hisar district, Haryana (2); Bhirrana is in Haryana's Fatehabad (4); Bhogavo is a river of Saurashtra (1). So A-3, B-2, C-4, D-1.

subject: History
stem: The Harappan site at Kot Diji is close to which one of the following major sites of that civilization?
explanation: Kot Diji, an Early Harappan site in Sindh, lies on the left bank of the Indus close to Mohenjo-daro. It is not near Harappa (Punjab), Lothal (Gujarat) or Kalibangan (Rajasthan).

subject: History
stem: From which one of the following factory sites were limestone and chert blades mass produced and sent to various Harappan settlements in Sindh?
explanation: The Sukkur and Rohri hills in Sindh were a major source of fine chert, where blades were mass-produced and distributed across Harappan Sindh. Khetri was a copper source; the Chagai/Baluchistan hills were not the blade factories.

subject: History
stem: In the citadel and lower-town areas of which one of the following Harappan cities, have several large cisterns and reservoirs been found?
explanation: Dholavira (Gujarat) is celebrated for its sophisticated water management — large rock-cut reservoirs and cisterns that stored monsoon and stream water in an arid setting. The other cities are not noted for these large reservoirs.

subject: History
stem: Which one among the following motifs does not appear on Harappan seals?
explanation: Harappan seals depict the humped bull, elephant, rhinoceros, tiger, crocodile and the mythical "unicorn" — but the lion is absent (lions were apparently not part of their iconography). Hence the lion.

subject: History
stem: Which one of the following periods has provided the evidence of a ploughed field at Kalibangan in Rajasthan?
explanation: Kalibangan (Rajasthan) yielded the famous ploughed field with criss-cross furrows — among the earliest evidence of ploughing in the world — and it dates to the Early (pre-Harappan) phase.

subject: History
stem: Consider the following statements:
subs: Statement I: The early Aryans, who were essentially pastoral, did not develop any political structure which could measure up to a State in either ancient or modern sense.

Statement II: As tribal Kingship was the same as chiefship, the term Rajan being used for tribal chief who was primarily a military leader and who ruled over his people and not over any specified area.
options: Both the statements are individually true and Statement II is the correct explanation of Statement I
Both the statements are individually true but Statement II is not the correct explanation of Statement I
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
explanation: Early Vedic society was tribal and pastoral, with no territorial State (I). The reason is that the Rajan was just a tribal war-leader who ruled over his people (jana), not over a defined territory — so no State machinery emerged (II). Statement II thus explains statement I.

subject: History
stem: Match List-I with List-II and select the correct answer using the code given below the Lists: List-I (Land Type) List-II (Description) A. Urvara - 1. Land watered by a river B. Maru - 2. Fertile land C. Nadimatrika - 3. Land watered by rain D. Devamatrika - 4. Desert land Code: A B C D
explanation: Urvara = fertile land (2), Maru = desert (4), Nadimatrika = land watered by a river (1), Devamatrika = land watered by rain (3). The correct code is therefore “2, 4, 1, 3,” which matches the original paper’s option (d); the option strings printed in this compilation do not match, so verify against the original.

subject: History
stem: In the 10th Mandala of the Rigveda, which one of the following hymns reflects upon the marriage ceremonies?
explanation: The Surya Sukta describes the marriage of Surya (the Sun-god's daughter) and is the Rig Vedic hymn associated with marriage rites. The Purusha Sukta deals with the origin of the four varnas, and the Dana Stutis praise gift-giving — not marriage.

subject: History
stem: At the banks of which of the following rivers was the Battle of Ten Kings fought in the Early Vedic Period?
explanation: The Dasarajna (Battle of Ten Kings), in which the Bharata king Sudas defeated a confederacy of ten tribes, was fought on the Parushni (modern Ravi). The other rivers are Vedic but not the site of this battle.

subject: History
stem: The terms used for cereals in the Rig Veda are
explanation: The Rig Veda mentions Yava (barley) and Dhanya (grain) — barley was the main early Vedic cereal. Godhuma (wheat) and Vrihi (rice) appear only in the later Vedic texts, so option a is not Rig Vedic.

subject: History
stem: In the Rig Veda Samhita, use of the terms ishukrit and isukara referred to the maker of
explanation: Ishu means "arrow," so ishukrit/isukara denote an arrow-maker — evidence of craft specialisation even in Vedic society. They do not refer to makers of swords, the vajra or maces.

subject: History
stem: Which one of the following statements about Rig Vedic goddesses is correct?
explanation: Sinivali is the Rig Vedic goddess of fertility and childbirth, who bestows children. Ushas is the goddess of dawn (not Aditi), and Aditi is the mother of the gods — so the other pairings are wrong.

subject: History
stem: Rakshasa type of marriage is
explanation: Of the eight forms of marriage in the Dharmashastras, Rakshasa is marriage by the forcible abduction/capture of the bride (a warrior practice). Marriage by purchase is Asura, and marriage by stealthy seduction is Paishacha.

subject: History
stem: According to the Manusmriti, women can acquire wealth through which of the following means?
explanation: The Manusmriti recognised women's stridhana — property received chiefly as gifts/tokens of affection (from parents, husband, relatives at marriage and other occasions). Independent purchase, investment or full inheritance were not the recognised routes for a woman's own wealth.

subject: History
stem: Which of the following rulers were identified through matronymics -- names derived from that of the mother?
explanation: The Satavahana kings used matronymics — names formed from the mother's name, e.g. Gautamiputra Satakarni, Vasishthiputra Pulumavi — pointing to the importance of the mother's lineage. The other groups did not characteristically do so.

subject: History
stem: Which one of the following statements on the issue of ownership according to Smriti literature is not correct?
options: The paternal estate was to be divided equally amongst sons
Women were allowed to retain the gifts they received on the occasion of their marriage
The gifts to women on the occasion of their marriage were known as Stridhana
Stridhana was not inherited by the children without the permission of the husband
explanation: Stridhana was a woman's absolute property, and on her death it passed to her children (especially daughters) — it did not require the husband's permission — so statement d is false. Equal division of the paternal estate among sons, women keeping marriage gifts, and those gifts being called stridhana are all correct.

subject: History
stem: From among the following Mahajanapadas, identify the gana/sangha (oligarchy):
explanation: The Vajji (Vrijji) confederacy, centred on Vaishali, was a gana-sangha — an oligarchic republic governed by an assembly of clan chiefs (notably the Licchavis). Magadha, Avanti and Kosala were monarchies.

subject: History
stem: On the banks of which one of the following rivers was the ancient Mahishmati located?
explanation: Mahishmati, an ancient capital (of the Avanti region / later the Haihayas and Kalachuris), stood on the Narmada. It was not on the Sarayu, Son or Godavari.

subject: History
stem: Consider the following statements:
subs: Statement I: King Ashoka abolished capital punishment and disbanded his army.
Statement II: After Kalinga War, Ashoka was remorseful and became a Buddhist.
options: Both the statements are individually true and Statement II is the correct explanation of Statement I
Both the statements are individually true but Statement II is not the correct explanation of Statement I
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
explanation: The Kalinga War (c. 261 BCE) turned Ashoka to remorse and Buddhism, recorded in his Rock Edict XIII — so II is true. But he did not abolish capital punishment or disband his army; he kept the death penalty (with only a three-day respite) and the Mauryan army/administration intact. His "conquest by Dhamma" changed policy, not state power. So I is false.

subject: History
stem: Which Buddhist text contains an account of the Mauryan Emperor Ashoka?
explanation: The Mahavamsa, the great Sri Lankan Pali chronicle, gives a detailed account of Ashoka and the spread of Buddhism. The three Pitakas are the canonical "baskets" of doctrine and discipline, not historical chronicles of Ashoka.

subject: History
stem: Consider the following statements:
subs: 1. According to Mahavamsa, Ashoka turned to the Buddha's dhamma when his nephew Nigrodha preached the doctrine to him.
2. Divyavadana ascribes Ashoka being drawn to the Buddha's teaching to the influence of Samudra, a merchant-turned monk.
3. Dipavamsa speaks of Samudra, the 12-year-old son of a merchant, as the key figure in Ashoka's coming under the influence of the Buddhist dhamma. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: Different traditions explain Ashoka's conversion differently: the Mahavamsa credits the young monk Nigrodha (1), and the Divyavadana credits the monk Samudra (2). Statement 3 misattributes the Samudra story to the Dipavamsa, so it is wrong. Hence 1 and 2.

subject: History
stem: Which one of the following was not a part of Kautilya's Saptanga Theory of the State?
explanation: Kautilya's Saptanga (seven limbs of the state) are Swami (king), Amatya (ministers), Janapada (territory/people), Durga (fort), Kosha (treasury), Danda (army) and Mitra (ally). Dhamma (Ashoka's moral code) is not one of the seven limbs.

subject: History
stem: Consider the following statements about Ashoka's faith in Buddhism:
subs: 1. Rummindei Pillar Inscription and Nigali Sagar Pillar Inscription provide clear evidence of Ashoka's faith.
2. Minor Rock Edict-I gives evidence of a sudden change in Ashoka's faith.
explanation: Ashoka's link with Buddhism is strongly proved by inscriptions like Rummindei and Nigali Sagar. At Rummindei he records his visit to Lumbini, the birthplace of the Buddha; at Nigali Sagar he records enlarging the stupa of Konakamana Buddha. These inscriptions show personal devotion and royal patronage of Buddhist sacred sites. Statement 2 is not correct because Minor Rock Edict I does not show a sudden change of faith - it shows Ashoka's Buddhist commitment growing over time.

subject: History
stem: Who among the following Indian thinkers first spoke of food-for-work programmes to counteract the effects of famines?
explanation: Kautilya, in the Arthashastra, advised famine-relief measures including state employment/food-for-work, distribution of seeds and food, and remission of taxes. Aryabhata (astronomy), Chandragomin (grammar) and Prabhakara (philosophy) dealt with other fields.

subject: History
stem: Consider the following statements about the Mauryan State and the forest people:
subs: 1. The forest people were subjected to new forms of political and economic dominance and the necessity to subordinate and assimilate them led to a change in the earlier attitude.
2. The State recognised that the forest produce was the sole monopoly of the forest people.
3. The State was concerned with conservation of forests and prohibited burning of forests.
4. The forest people could be harnessed to serve the State as troops, spies and assassins.
explanation: Statements 1, 3 and 4 are correct — the State asserted control over forest peoples, protected forests (banning burning), and used them as troops/spies. Statement 2 is wrong — forest produce was the State's monopoly, not the forest people's. So three statements are correct.

subject: History
stem: Which of the following is/are the correct components of the Lion Capital at Sarnath?
subs: 1. The shaft (stambha-yashti) or the tall tapering column.
2. An elliptical top part (the abacus), devoid of any carvings.
3. Overflowing inverted petals below the abacus.
explanation: The capital sits on the tall shaft/column (1) and rests on an inverted lotus (bell) base (3). Statement 2 is wrong — the abacus is richly carved (with the four animals and chakras), not "devoid of carvings." Hence 1 and 3 only.

subject: History
stem: Consider the following pairs of High Offices and concerned Departments in the Mauryan Administration:
subs: 1. Samahartri -- Chief Collector of Revenue
2. Dauvarika -- Chief of Army
3. Akshapatala -- Records-cum-Audit
4. Samnidhatri -- Treasurer Officer.
explanation: Samahartri (chief revenue collector, 1), Akshapatala (records and audit, 3) and Samnidhatri (treasurer, 4) are correctly matched. Pair 2 is wrong — Dauvarika was the chief of the palace guards/door-keeper, while the army chief was the Senapati. Hence 1, 3 and 4 only.

subject: History
stem: Which one of the following statements about Ashoka's inscriptions is correct?
options: The Major Rock Edict found in Kerala is in Sanskrit
Minor Rock Edicts in Bihar are found only in Pataliputra
The Dhauli Major Rock Edict makes a mention of the Kalinga War
Kalsi in Uttarakhand is a site of Major Rock
explanation: Kalsi (Uttarakhand) is a well-known Major Rock Edict site (d, correct). The others are wrong: there is no major rock edict in Kerala (and Ashokan edicts are in Prakrit, not Sanskrit); Bihar's minor rock edict is at Sasaram (not Pataliputra); and the Dhauli/Jaugada (Kalinga) edicts actually omit the Kalinga-war edict (RE XIII).

subject: History
stem: Which one of the following present day territories was outside the limits of Ashoka's empire?
explanation: Ashoka's empire stretched from Afghanistan/Baluchistan to Bengal and Karnataka, including Kashmir and North Bengal. The far north-east — the Brahmaputra valley (Assam/Kamarupa) — lay outside its limits.

subject: History
stem: Which of the following statements about sculptures at Sanchi is/are correct?
subs: 1. Some sculptures show Shalabhanjika.
2. The Shalabhanjika figure is represented by a woman surrounded by lotuses and elephants.
3. The Shalabhanjika motif was not directly inspired by Buddhist ideas.
explanation: Sanchi does carry Shalabhanjika figures — a woman grasping a tree, a folk fertility/auspicious motif — and this motif came from popular tradition, not Buddhist doctrine (1 and 3 correct). Statement 2 actually describes Gajalakshmi (the goddess flanked by elephants and lotuses), not the Shalabhanjika — so 2 is wrong. Hence 1 and 3 only.

subject: History
stem: Consider the following statements about Kanaganahalli archaeological site:
subs: 1. It is situated on the bank of Bhima river.
2. The remains of the Kanaganahalli Stupa can be dated between 1st and 3rd centuries CE.
3. Emperor Ashoka's image was found at the site.
explanation: Kanaganahalli (Karnataka) lies on the Bhima river (1), its stupa belongs to the 1st–3rd centuries CE (2), and it yielded a rare sculpted portrait labelled "Raya Asoko" (King Ashoka) (3). All three are correct.

subject: History
stem: In the decoration of Stupas, what does the term Kinjalkini refer to?
options: A garland of thousand lotus flowers
explanation: Kinjalka means a lotus filament/petal, and Kinjalkini denotes a decorative garland of a thousand lotus flowers used to ornament stupas. It is not creepers, scarves or mango pendants.

subject: History
stem: The antariya was a garment worn by people during the Mauryan period. It was
options: an unstitched length of cloth draped around the body
a sewn garment consisting of a sleeved tunic
a head covering used exclusively by women
made of strips sewn together tied at the waist with a cord
explanation: The antariya was the lower garment of ancient India — an unstitched cloth wrapped around the lower body (with the upper cloth called uttariya). Stitched/sleeved tunics and head coverings are not the antariya.

subject: History
stem: Consider the following pairs (Historical Place -- Site): I. Girnar -- Sudarshana Lake II. Cholistan -- Terracotta model of Plough III. Mant near Mathura -- Headless standing figure of Kanishka IV. Sannati -- Asokan Pillar Edict in Odisha. How many pairs given above is/are correctly matched?
explanation: Three pairs are correct: Girnar had the Sudarshana Lake (repaired by various rulers) (I); Cholistan yielded a terracotta plough model (II); and the headless Kanishka statue is from Mant near Mathura (III). Pair IV is wrong — Sannati (with Ashokan edicts) is in Karnataka, not Odisha. So 3 are correct.

subject: History
stem: Which of the following statements with regard to the heterodox sect between sixth and fourth century BC are correct?
subs: 1. The Jain ideas were already being circulated in the seventh century BC by Parshva.
2. Although Buddhism, and to a lesser extent Jainism, took account of the changes in material life and reacted against orthodoxy, neither of these sought to abolish the caste system.
3. The first female disciple of Mahavira is said to have been a captured slave woman.
4. Buddha held that nuns could attain spiritual liberation just like a monk and granted them an equal status in the mendicant order.
explanation: Jainism predates Mahavira — the 23rd Tirthankara Parshva spread its ideas in the 7th c. BCE (1). Both faiths challenged Vedic ritualism but did not actually abolish caste (2), and Mahavira's first woman disciple, Chandanbala, was a captured slave (3). Statement 4 is wrong — the Buddha admitted nuns reluctantly, under eight subordinating rules, so they did not get equal status. Hence 1, 2 and 3 only.

subject: History
stem: Which one of the following statements about Buddhist Stupas in India is not correct?
options: Ashoka played an important role in popularizing the Stupa cult.
They were repositories of relics of Buddha and other monks.
They were located in rural areas.
They were located close to trade routes.
explanation: Stupas were deliberately built near towns and trade routes (so pilgrims and merchants could visit and donate), not in remote rural areas — so statement c is false. Ashoka did popularise the stupa cult, and stupas did hold relics, which are correct.

subject: History
stem: The dialogue on Varna between king Avantiputta and Kachchana, a disciple of Buddha, appears in which one of the following Buddhist texts?
explanation: This caste dialogue (the Madhura Sutta) is in the Majjhima Nikaya (the "middle-length discourses"). It is not in the Samyutta or Anguttara Nikaya, nor in the Ambattha Sutta.

subject: History
stem: Who among the following was not a Jain Acharya?
explanation: Khema was a chief Buddhist nun (a foremost female disciple of the Buddha), not a Jain Acharya. Bhadrabahu (who led the Digambaras south) and Haribhadra (a Jain scholar-monk) were Jain Acharyas.

subject: History
stem: Which one of the following statements about the Shvetambara Sect of Jainism is not correct?
options: It refers to monks, who wear white clothes
It refers to monks, who migrated to the south of Vindhyas in 4th century BCE
They recognized the existence of canonical texts including the 12 Angas
They believed that women can also attain salvation
explanation: It was the Digambaras (under Bhadrabahu) who migrated south during a famine; the Shvetambaras ("white-clad") stayed in the north — so statement b is false. The Shvetambaras do wear white, accept the 12 Angas, and believe women can achieve liberation (all correct).

subject: History
stem: Which one of the following statements about the Ajivikas is not correct?
options: They did not practise discrimination on the basis of caste or class
They had a corporate organisation and canonical texts
They practised severe asceticism, often eating very little food
They formed an alliance with Jainas against Buddhists
explanation: The Ajivikas (founded by Makkhali Gosala, believers in strict fatalism/niyati) did not form an anti-Buddhist alliance with the Jainas — so statement d is false. They did reject caste, had an organised order and texts, and practised extreme asceticism.

subject: History
stem: Who among the following were referred to as the 'Theris' in ancient India?
options: Women expelled from the Buddhist Sangha
explanation: Theris were the senior/elder Buddhist nuns; their devotional verses form the Therigatha, one of the earliest collections of women's writing. The term does not mean respected women generally, or denied/expelled women.

subject: History
stem: Which one of the following statements about the Buddhist Goddess Hariti is correct?
options: She was converted by the Buddha from a blood-thirsty yakshi to a benevolent maternal figure
She is the feminine personification of wisdom
She protects people from eight great fears
She is a warrior goddess associated with the dawn
explanation: Hariti was a child-devouring yakshi whom the Buddha, by teaching her a parent's grief, converted into a protector of children and mothers. She is not a wisdom personification, a saviour-from-eight-fears (that is Tara), or a dawn warrior.

subject: History
stem: The epic, Silappadikaram refers to the
explanation: The Tamil epic Silappadikaram, by the Jain prince-ascetic Ilango Adigal, is shot through with Jaina ideas — karma, fate and renunciation shape the story of Kovalan and Kannagi. (Though it ends with Kannagi's deification, the exam's keyed answer is its Jaina element.)

subject: History
stem: Which river is praised in the fifth century Tamil epic, Silappadikaram?
explanation: The epic glorifies the Cauvery (Kaveri), the great river of the Chola country where much of the tale unfolds. The Godavari, Saraswati and Ganges are not celebrated in it.

subject: History
stem: Match List-I with List-II and select the correct answer using the code given below the Lists: List-I (Sangam Tinai) List-II (Region) A. Kurinchi - 1. Pastoral region B. Palai - 2. Seashore C. Neytal - 3. Arid region D. Mullai - 4. Mountainous region Code: A B C D
explanation: Sangam poetry classifies land into five tinais: Kurinchi = mountainous (4), Palai = arid/desert (3), Neytal = seashore (2), Mullai = pastoral/forest (1). So A-4, B-3, C-2, D-1.

subject: History
stem: Who is the author of Manimekalai?
explanation: Manimekalai, the Buddhist Tamil epic (a sequel to the Silappadikaram), was composed by Sittalai Sathanar. Ilango Adigal wrote the Silappadikaram; Kovalan is a character in it.

subject: History
stem: The story Gandatindu Jataka was written in which language?
explanation: The Jataka tales (stories of the Buddha's previous births), including the Gandatindu Jataka, are part of the Buddhist canon written in Pali.

subject: History
stem: According to the Tamil Sangam texts, who among the following were the large landowners?
explanation: In Sangam society the Vellalars were the wealthy land-owning agriculturist class. Uzhavars were ordinary cultivators/ploughmen, Adimais were labourers/slaves, and Gahapati is a northern term.

subject: History
stem: The famous Hathigumpha inscription of Kharavela mentions that in the eleventh year of his reign, Kharavela destroyed confederacy of Tamil States, Tramiradesa Sanghatam, which had long been a source of danger. How old was this confederacy of Tamil States at that time?
explanation: Kharavela's Hathigumpha inscription (Udayagiri, Odisha) records that in his 11th regnal year he broke the Tramira (Tamil) confederacy, which had existed for 113 years.

subject: History
stem: Match List I (Ethnic Territorial Segment) with List II (Related Occupational Pattern): A. Maruta Makkal B. Kuravan Makkal C. Mullai Makkal D. Neytal Makkal -- 1. Pastoralists 2. Fishing people 3. Ploughmen 4. Hill people.
explanation: Each Sangam eco-zone had its people: Marutam (plains) → ploughmen (3), Kuravan (hills) → hill people (4), Mullai (forest/pasture) → pastoralists (1), Neytal (coast) → fishing people (2). So A-3, B-4, C-1, D-2.

subject: History
stem: Which one of the following was an important Pandya port, celebrated for its pearls in Sangam poems and Greek accounts?
explanation: Korkai, on the Pandya coast (near the Gulf of Mannar), was the famous pearl-fishery port noted in Sangam literature and Greco-Roman accounts. Muchiri (Muziris) was Chera, Puhar was Chola, and Arikamedu was a Tamil-Roman trading site.

subject: History
stem: The Tolkappiyam is
options: A Tamil poem in praise of Rajendra Chola
An ancient didactic work in Tamil
A drama composed by a Chera King
explanation: The Tolkappiyam is the oldest surviving Tamil work — a treatise on grammar and poetics. It is not a praise-poem, an ethical/didactic text (that is the Tirukkural) or a drama.

subject: History
stem: Consider the following statements about Madurai, the capital city of the Pandya kingdom:
subs: 1. It has been described in Maduraikkanchi as a large grand city, enclosed by walls on three sides and the Vaigai river on the fourth side.
2. The Arthashastra mentions it as a centre of fine cotton textiles.
3. Other literary sources describe it as a major craft centre.
explanation: The Sangam poem Maduraikkanchi paints Madurai as a great walled city by the Vaigai (1); Kautilya's Arthashastra notes "Madhura" cotton fabrics (2); and other texts describe its crafts (3). All three are correct.

subject: History
stem: What form of Shiva is most prominent in the Brihadeshvara Temple built by the Chola dynasty?
explanation: Rajaraja I's Brihadeshvara (Rajarajeshvara) Temple at Thanjavur prominently depicts Shiva as Tripurantaka — the destroyer of the three demon cities — reflecting the king's martial, imperial image. Harihara, Bhairava and Rudra are not its dominant form.

subject: History
stem: Which one of the following pairs of terms and their meanings is not correctly matched?
options: Nagarakkani -- Land owned by the nagaram
Viragal -- Hero stone in Tamil Nadu
explanation: Nattar were the members/assembly of the nadu (a rural territorial unit) in Chola administration — not a serpent deity, so this pair is wrong. Nagarakkani (town-guild land), Yupa (Vedic sacrificial post) and Viragal (memorial hero-stone) are correctly matched.

subject: History
stem: Consider the following statements with reference to the Nataraj bronze sculpture of Chola period:
subs: 1. Shiva is shown balancing himself on his right leg representing tirobhava.
2. Shiva is shown raising his left leg in bhujangatrasita stance, representing kicking away the illusion from the devotee's mind.
3. The main right hand holds the damaru, Shiva's favourite musical instrument.
explanation: In the Nataraja, the planted right foot (on the dwarf Apasmara) signifies tirobhava (veiling) (1), the raised left leg in the bhujangatrasita pose signifies liberation/removal of illusion (2), and an upper right hand holds the damaru drum of creation (3). All three correctly describe the icon.

subject: History
stem: Which one of the following has been an important early Chola painting discovered at the Brihadisvara Temple, Thanjavur in 1931?
options: Buddha with Chauri-bearers on either side
Parshvanatha with snake-hood on a lion-throne
explanation: Hidden beneath later Nayaka murals, the Chola frescoes discovered in 1931 are devoted to Shiva — including the noted Shiva as Yoga-Dakshinamurti. The Buddha, Yakshi Kali and Parshvanatha belong to Buddhist/Jain art, not these Shaiva Chola paintings.

subject: History
stem: Who among the following Chola kings encouraged the Sailendra ruler of Sri Vijaya to build a Buddhist Vihara in Negapatnam?
explanation: Rajaraja I permitted the Sailendra (Srivijaya) king to build the Chudamani Vihara at Nagapattinam, reflecting Chola–Southeast Asian trade and diplomatic ties. The other Chola kings were not associated with this grant.

subject: History
stem: Consider the following statements about Ancient Hindu metal images in India:
subs: 1. The Chola period is well known for the aesthetic and technical finesse of its metal sculpture.
2. The iconography and style of Chola metal images were similar to those of their stone counterparts.
explanation: Chola bronzes (cast by the lost-wax method) are world-famous for their grace and technical perfection (1), and they followed the same iconographic conventions and style as the temple stone sculptures (2). Both are correct.

subject: History
stem: Name the location of the musical inscription containing groups of musical notes arranged for the benefit of his pupils by a king in the seventh or eighth century CE.
explanation: The Kudumiyamalai (Tamil Nadu) rock inscription records groups of musical notes (an early teaching aid for music). Uttaramerur is famous for its assembly (sabha) inscription, and Ennayiram for an educational institution — not for this musical text.

subject: History
stem: Which one of the following pairs is not correctly matched?
explanation: In Tamil land terminology Nancai = wet (irrigated) fields, Puncai = dry fields and Tottakal = garden lands — all correct. The pairing "Kuddapah-kar — rocky wastelands" is the odd, incorrectly matched one.

subject: History
stem: Which of the following dynasties of South India issued their documents first in Prakrit and later in Sanskrit?
options: The Pandyas of the post-Sangam Age
explanation: The Pallavas of Tondaimandalam issued their early copper-plate charters in Prakrit and shifted to Sanskrit in later grants — a marker of the growing prestige of Sanskrit in the south.

subject: History
stem: The royal portraits of Simhavishnu and Mahendravarman are found in which cave temple at Mamallapuram?
explanation: The Adivaraha cave temple at Mamallapuram (Mahabalipuram) contains sculpted royal portraits of the Pallava kings Simhavishnu and Mahendravarman with their queens — rare contemporary royal images. The Varaha, Ramanuja and Trimurti caves do not carry these portraits.

subject: History
stem: The notion of kinship projected by the Kushana rulers is best evidenced through which of the following?
explanation: The Kushanas projected their idea of divine kingship mainly through coins (bearing deities and grand royal titles like devaputra) and sculpture (the devakula shrine statues of kings at Mat near Mathura). Grants and panegyric inscriptions were less central to their self-image.

subject: History
stem: Harshacharita has references to various presents sent by a ruler named Bhaskara to Harshavardhana. Bhaskara belonged to
explanation: Bhaskaravarman of the Varman dynasty of Kamarupa (Assam) was Harsha's ally against Shashanka of Bengal, and the Harshacharita records the gifts he sent. The Haryanka and Nanda dynasties belonged to a much earlier era.

subject: History
stem: Which one of the following statements about the Gupta period in Indian History is not correct?
options: Sanskrit language and literature, after centuries of evolution, reached what has been described as a level of classical excellence through royal patronage.

The status of women was redefined. They were entitled to formal education and hence there were women teachers, philosophers and doctors. Early marriage was prohibited by law and they were given the right to property.
Decentralization of administrative authority was impacted by increased grants of land and villages with fiscal and administrative immunities to priests and temples.
Land grants paved the way for feudal developments and emergence of serfdom in India, resulting in the depression of the peasantry.
explanation: In the Gupta age the position of women actually declined — early marriage spread, widow remarriage and property rights shrank, and education was largely denied to women. So statement b is false. The flourishing of classical Sanskrit and the feudal/decentralising effects of land grants are correct.

subject: History
stem: Who among the following usurped power in Kanyakubja after the death of Harshavardhana?
explanation: After Harsha's death his minister Arunashva (Arjuna) seized power at Kanyakubja and even attacked the Chinese envoy Wang Hsuan-tse — who, with Tibetan/Nepali help, defeated and captured him. Shashanka (Bengal) and Lalitaditya (Kashmir) belonged to other regions/times.

subject: History
stem: Which one of the following statements about Gupta coins is not correct?
options: Gupta kings issued large number of gold coins known as Dinar
Chandragupta II, Kumaragupta I, Skandagupta and Budhagupta issued silver coins
Obverses of coins are carved with images of the kings and the reverse with deities
The largest number of coins issued by the Guptas were of copper
explanation: The Guptas are famous above all for their gold coins (Dinaras) — they issued the largest number and finest of these, not copper coins — so statement d is false. They did also strike silver coins, and the obverse-king/reverse-deity design is correct.

subject: History
stem: Which one of the following ancient Indian kings was praised in glowing terms in the Prayaga Prashasti?
explanation: The Prayaga Prashasti (Allahabad Pillar inscription), composed by the court poet Harisena, is a eulogy of Samudragupta, detailing his conquests. (Interestingly it is engraved on the same pillar that bears Ashoka's edicts.)

subject: History
stem: Where is the famous Gupta period Deogarh Temple situated?
explanation: The Dashavatara Temple at Deogarh, an early Gupta stone temple (with the famous Vishnu Anantashayana panel), is in the Lalitpur district of Uttar Pradesh.

subject: History
stem: Consider the following statements about Ajanta Cave paintings:
subs: 1. The paintings depict yakshas, gandharvas and apsaras.
2. The artists have used the technique of providing multiple perspectives.
explanation: The Ajanta murals (Buddhist, Gupta-Vakataka era) portray a rich world of yakshas, gandharvas and apsaras alongside Jataka scenes (1), and the painters skilfully used multiple viewpoints/perspectives to convey depth and movement (2). Both statements are correct.

subject: History
stem: Which of the following Gods are also known as Lokapalas or the Guardians of the Universe?
explanation: The Lokapalas/Dikpalas guard the directions: Indra (east), Yama (south), Varuna (west) and Kubera (north). The other options wrongly bring in Skanda, Brahma or Shiva.

subject: History
stem: Which of the following statements about the philosopher Shankara is/are true?
subs: 1. Shankara espoused form of Vedanta called Advaita.
2. He elaborated on the philosophy of Gaudapada.
3. Shankara tried to demonstrate that the Upanishads and Brahmasutras contain a unified, systematic philosophy.
4. Shankara founded the Amanaya Mathas.
explanation: Shankara taught Advaita (non-dualism) (1), built on Gaudapada's Mandukya Karika (2), and argued that the Upanishads/Brahmasutras form a single coherent system (3). Statement 4 (founding the four mathas) is the one left out by the options, giving 1, 2 and 3.

subject: History
stem: Who was/were the 10th century composers of the Nalayira Divya Prabandham?
explanation: The Nalayira Divya Prabandham ("Four Thousand Divine Hymns") is the collected Tamil devotional poetry of the Alvars (Vaishnava saints), compiled by Nathamuni. Appar and Sambandar were Nayanars (Shaiva saints).

subject: History
stem: Which of the following is/are the features of the Brahmadeya Grants during c 600-1200 AD?
subs: 1. Their creation meant a renunciation of actual or potential sources of revenue by the State.
2. These grants could vary from a small plot to several villages.
3. Most grants were made in unsettled areas. Select the correct answer using the code given below
explanation: A brahmadeya was tax-free land granted to Brahmins, so the State gave up revenue (1), and grants varied from a plot to several villages (2). Statement 3 is wrong — such grants were typically in fertile, settled agrarian areas (and also to extend cultivation), not "mostly unsettled." Hence 1 and 2 only.

subject: History
stem: Which one of the following statements about the Elephanta Island is correct?
options: It was given its name by the British after a large elephant structure located there.
It is well-known for a spectacular carving of Vishnu described in the Vishnudharmottara Purana.
It is associated with the Pashupata sect.
explanation: The Elephanta caves are Shaiva and linked with the Pashupata sect of Shaivism (d, correct). The island was named by the Portuguese (not British); it has several caves; and its masterpiece is the great Shiva (Trimurti/Sadashiva), not Vishnu.

subject: History
stem: Which one among the following is a temple of the Vaishnavite tradition?
explanation: Srirangam (Tiruchirapalli) is dedicated to Vishnu as Ranganatha — one of the holiest Vaishnava shrines. Chidambaram (Nataraja), Gangaikonda Cholapuram and Thanjavur (Brihadeshvara) are Shaiva temples.

subject: History
stem: To which one of the following schools of philosophy did Kumarila Bhatta Prabhakara belong?
explanation: Kumarila Bhatta and Prabhakara were the two great exponents of Purva-Mimamsa (which emphasises Vedic ritual and the authority of the Vedas). Lokayata is materialism, Madhyamika is a Buddhist school, and Uttara-Mimamsa is Vedanta.

subject: History
stem: Which among the following are Charvaka Schools of Philosophy?
subs: 1. Dhurtta
2. Nyaya
3. Sushikshita
4. Vaisheshika.
explanation: The Charvaka (Lokayata) materialist tradition had sub-schools including Dhurtta and Sushikshita (1 and 3). Nyaya and Vaisheshika are orthodox (astika) schools, not Charvaka. Hence 1 and 3 only.

subject: History
stem: Which one of the following pairs denoting various forms of Bhakti is not correctly matched?
options: Saguna -- Belief in Gods with attributes
Nirguna -- Belief in Gods without attributes
Alvars -- Believed in devotion of Shakti
Nayanars -- Believed in devotion of Shiva
explanation: The Alvars were devotees of Vishnu, not Shakti — so this pair is wrong. Saguna (with form/attributes), Nirguna (formless) and the Nayanars (Shaiva devotees of Shiva) are correctly matched.

subject: History
stem: Who among the following is not a woman Nayanar saint?
explanation: Andal was an Alvar (a Vaishnava woman saint), not a Nayanar. Karaikkal Ammaiyar, Mangayarkkarasiyar and Isaignaniyar were the three women among the 63 Shaiva Nayanars.

subject: History
stem: Who were the Nayanars?
options: Those who were immersed in devotion to Vishnu
Those who were devotees of Buddha
Leaders who were devotees of Shiva
explanation: The Nayanars were the 63 Tamil Shaiva saints, devotees of Shiva, whose hymns form the Tevaram. (The Vaishnava counterparts were the Alvars.)

subject: History
stem: Who were Alvars?
options: Those who immersed in devotion to Vishnu
Those who worshipped abstract form of God
explanation: The Alvars were the Tamil Vaishnava poet-saints, absorbed in loving devotion to Vishnu (their hymns form the Divya Prabandham). The Shaiva devotees were the Nayanars.

subject: History
stem: Consider the following statements about the Vedanta philosophy:
subs: 1. Shankara was the founder of Advaita Vedanta.
2. The philosophy of Dvaita was elaborated by Madhva.
explanation: Shankara propounded Advaita (non-dualism — the soul and Brahman are one) (1), while Madhva developed Dvaita (dualism — soul and God are eternally distinct) (2). Both statements are correct.

subject: History
stem: Why are the Gypsies regarded as of Indian origin?
subs: 1. They follow Indian religious practices.
2. Their language, called Romani, is an Indo-Aryan language.
3. They believe that they came from India.
4. Epigraphic evidence locates their original home in Uttar Pradesh.
explanation: The strongest evidence is linguistic — Romani is an Indo-Aryan language (2) — supported by the Gypsies' own tradition that they migrated from India (3). They do not follow Indian religious practices, and there is no epigraphic "home in UP" evidence — so 1 and 4 are wrong.

subject: History
stem: In the first century AD, which among the following was not a major item of Indian exports to Rome?
explanation: India exported pepper, spices like spikenard, tortoiseshell, pearls and textiles to Rome. Nutmeg came from the Moluccas (Indonesia), not India, so it was not a major Indian export then.

subject: History
stem: Name the site that gives us valuable information about India's maritime links on the Coromandel coast.
explanation: Arikamedu, near Puducherry, is the key archaeological site for Indo-Roman maritime trade on the Coromandel (east) coast, yielding Roman pottery, amphorae and beads. Bharukachchha was a west-coast port; Anuradhapura is in Sri Lanka.

subject: History
stem: Consider the following statements:
subs: Statement I: The Greek travellers were most impressed by the fertility of India's soil and the energy and ability of her cultivators.
Statement II: Ancient India knew the use of manure.
explanation: Both facts are true, but knowing manure does not by itself explain why the Greeks admired India's soil and cultivators — that admiration rested mainly on natural fertility and farming effort. So II is true but not the cause of I.

subject: History
stem: In the Gandhara School of Art, initially blue schist and green phyllite were used. When did stucco completely replace stone as main material used by Gandhara School sculptors?
explanation: Early Gandhara sculpture used blue schist and green phyllite (stone); from about the 3rd century CE cheaper, more workable stucco (lime plaster) largely replaced stone as the main medium. (Some sources stretch this into the 4th century, but 3rd century CE is the intended answer.)

subject: History
stem: Which twelfth century Sanskrit scholar was first responsible for the compilation of Nibandhas or digests of epic and Puranic texts?
explanation: Lakshmidhara, a minister of the Gahadavala king Govindachandra, compiled the Krityakalpataru, the first great Nibandha (legal/ritual digest) drawing on epic and Puranic sources. Harsha and Kalidasa were earlier literary figures, and Govindachandra was the patron-king, not the author.

subject: History
stem: Consider the following statements with reference to Paleolithic tools:
subs: 1. Isampur was a well-known centre of stone tool manufacture located in Gulbarga district of Karnataka and was situated along a small seasonal stream known as Kamta Halla.
2. A Paleolithic blade is a flake tool, the length of which is more than twice its width.
explanation: Isampur was indeed a Lower Palaeolithic tool-making site in Karnataka (1), and a blade is technically defined as a flake at least twice as long as it is wide (2). Both statements are correct.

subject: History
stem: Which one of the following statements about the famous text of Panchatantra is correct?
options: It is a philosophical text refuting rival positions
A text ushering in linguistics as a formal science
A text discussing developments in natural sciences
It is a text showing through illustration what should and should not be done
explanation: The Panchatantra is a collection of interlinked animal fables designed to teach niti (practical wisdom/statecraft) — it illustrates prudent and imprudent conduct through stories. It is not a work of philosophy, linguistics or natural science.

subject: History
stem: Consider the following statements about Patanjali's Mahabhashya:
subs: 1. It makes a mention of Kautilya.
2. It is a book on grammar and refers to historical personalities only incidentally.
explanation: The Mahabhashya is essentially a great commentary on Sanskrit grammar (on Panini, via Katyayana), touching on historical figures and events only in passing (2, correct). It does not mention Kautilya, so statement 1 is wrong.

subject: History
stem: Match List I (Literary Work) with List II (Author): A. Duta B. Mrichchhakatikam C. Kiratarjuniyam D. Ravanavadha -- 1. Ghatotkacha Bhatti 2. Bhasa 3. Shudraka 4. Bharavi.
explanation: Duta (Dutavakya/Dutaghatotkacha) is by Bhasa (2); Mrichchhakatikam ("The Little Clay Cart") by Shudraka (3); Kiratarjuniyam by Bharavi (4); and Ravanavadha (the Bhattikavya) by Bhatti (1). So A-2, B-3, C-4, D-1.

subject: History
stem: Which one of the following is not the work of Ashvaghosha?
explanation: Ashvaghosha (Kanishka's court poet) wrote the Buddhacharita, Saundarananda and the play Sariputraprakarana. The Manjushrimulakalpa is a later Mahayana/tantric text, not his work.

subject: History
stem: Which one among the following is the earliest text?
explanation: Panini's Ashtadhyayi (c. 5th–4th century BCE), the foundational Sanskrit grammar, is the earliest of these. The Natyashastra, Sushruta Samhita and Manusmriti were composed later (around the start of the Common Era).

subject: History
stem: Which one of the following statements about the grammarian Katyayana is correct?
options: He was a contemporary of Panini
He was a contemporary of Patanjali
He belonged to a period between Panini and Patanjali
He belonged to a period later than both Panini and Patanjali
explanation: The grammatical tradition runs Panini → Katyayana → Patanjali. Katyayana wrote the Vartikas (critical notes) on Panini's sutras, which Patanjali later explained in the Mahabhashya — so Katyayana sits between the two.

subject: History
stem: Which of the following can be considered a part of the ancient Indian Smriti Literature?
subs: 1. Puranas
2. Ramayana
3. Kalpa-Sutra
4. Vedas.
explanation: Smriti ("that which is remembered") includes the Puranas (1), the epics like the Ramayana (2) and the Kalpa-Sutras (Vedanga, 3). The Vedas are Shruti ("that which is heard" — revealed), not Smriti, so 4 is excluded. Hence 1, 2 and 3 only.

subject: History
stem: The legend of Prahlad and his father Hiranyakashyap was first set out in the
explanation: The Prahlada–Hiranyakashipu story (leading to the Narasimha avatar) was first narrated in the Vishnu Purana (one of the earliest Mahapuranas), and later elaborated in the Bhagavata Purana.

subject: History
stem: Which one of the following does not refer to the bow and arrow as a weapon of war in ancient India?
explanation: Sharnga (Vishnu's bow), Kodanda (Rama's bow) and Karmuka are all names for the bow. Kunta means a spear/lance, so it does not denote a bow and arrow.

subject: History
stem: Which of the following statements is/are correct?
subs: 1. The Bakshali Manuscript written in the Gatha language using the Sharada script deals with topics such as fractions, square roots, arithmetic and geometric progressions.
2. In geometry, Aryabhatta described properties of a circle giving a very accurate value for pi (correct to four decimal places) at 3.1416.
3. Sharangadhara Samhita is an important text on political system of ancient India.
explanation: The Bakhshali Manuscript is an early mathematical text (fractions, roots, progressions) (1), and Aryabhata computed pi ≈ 3.1416, remarkably accurate for his time (2). Statement 3 is wrong — the Sharangadhara Samhita is a medieval medical (Ayurvedic) text, not a treatise on polity. Hence 1 and 2 only.

subject: History
stem: In which one of the following states are the Guntupalle Buddhist Caves located?
explanation: The Guntupalle (Guntupalli) rock-cut Buddhist caves — notable for a circular chaitya hall — are in the Eluru district of Andhra Pradesh. They are not in Karnataka, Tamil Nadu or Kerala.

subject: History
stem: Who among the following Sultans succeeded in finally breaking and destroying the power of Turkan-i-Chihalgani?
explanation: The Turkan-i-Chihalgani ("the Forty") were a powerful corps of Turkish nobles created by Iltutmish who later dominated weak sultans. Balban systematically crushed them to centralise royal authority and exalt the throne. Alauddin and Muhammad bin Tughluq ruled later, after the Forty were gone.

subject: History
stem: Who among the following Mongol leaders/commanders did not cross Indus to attack India?
explanation: Genghis (Chenghiz) Khan reached the Indus (1221, chasing Jalaluddin Mangabarni) but turned back without crossing into India. The others led actual Mongol raids across the Indus during the Khalji/Tughluq period.

subject: History
stem: Ibn Batuta went to China as the envoy of which one of the following Delhi Sultans?
explanation: The Moroccan traveller Ibn Batuta served as a qazi at the court of Muhammad bin Tughluq, who appointed him envoy to the Mongol/Yuan court of China.

subject: History
stem: At which of the following places was diamond mining carried out during the Delhi Sultanate period?
explanation: Panna (in present-day Madhya Pradesh) is India's long-famous diamond-mining region, worked since medieval times. Awadh, Khambhat (a port) and Lakhnauti (Bengal capital) were not diamond sources.

subject: History
stem: In which one of the following works has Amir Khusrau praised the climate of India, its languages, notably Sanskrit, its arts and music and even its animals?
explanation: In Nuh Sipihr ("The Nine Skies"), Amir Khusrau celebrated India — its climate, languages including Sanskrit, arts, music and even its animals. The other works deal with rhetoric, a romance (Dewal Rani), or Alauddin's conquests (Khazainul Futuh).

subject: History
stem: Consider the following:
subs: 1. Tughlaqabad Fort
2. Bada Gumbad in Lodhi Garden
3. Qutub Minar
4. Fatehpur Sikri Which one of the following is the correct chronological order of building the above monuments?
explanation: By date: Qutub Minar (begun c. 1199, Slave dynasty) → Tughlaqabad Fort (1321, Tughlaqs) → Bada Gumbad (late 15th c., Lodis) → Fatehpur Sikri (1571, Akbar). So 3, 1, 2, 4.

subject: History
stem: The famous Mughal painting, depicting Jahangir embracing the Safavid king Shah Abbas, was painted by which one of the following Mughal painters?
explanation: The famous allegorical painting of Jahangir embracing Shah Abbas (standing on a lion and lamb over a globe) was by Abul Hasan ("Nadir-uz-Zaman"), Jahangir's favourite. Abd al-Samad and Dasavant served Akbar; Bishandas painted Shah Abbas from life but not this allegory.

subject: History
stem: The painted illustration of the moving of the Ashoka Pillar at Topra is found in
explanation: Firoz Shah Tughluq transported an Ashokan pillar from Topra to Delhi; the operation is illustrated in the Sirat-i-Firuz Shahi, a contemporary account of his reign. The Tarikh-i-Firuz Shahi (Barani/Afif) and the Akbar Nama do not carry this illustration.

subject: History
stem: Consider the following statements about Ellora:
subs: 1. This art-historical site is associated exclusively with Buddhism.
2. The Kailasa temple at Ellora has been built of dressed stone and kiln fired bricks.
explanation: Ellora is famous precisely because it has Buddhist, Hindu and Jain caves together — not "exclusively Buddhist" (1 wrong). The Kailasa temple is a single monolith carved top-down out of the living rock, not built of dressed stone and bricks (2 wrong). So neither statement is correct.

subject: History
stem: Verses ascribed to poet-saint Kabir have been compiled in which of the following traditions?
subs: 1. Bijak in Varanasi
2. Kabir Granthavali in Rajasthan
3. Adi Granth Sahib Select the correct answer using the code given below
explanation: Kabir's verses survive in three major traditions — the Bijak (with the Kabirpanth in Varanasi/east), the Kabir Granthavali (Rajasthan), and the Sikh Adi Granth Sahib. All three are correct.

subject: History
stem: Around twelfth century, Sufi Silsilas began to crystallize in different parts of the Islamic world to signify
subs: 1. continuous link between the master and disciple
2. unbroken spiritual genealogy to the Prophet Muhammad
3. the transmission of spiritual power and blessings to devotees Select the correct answer using the code given below
explanation: A silsila is a Sufi "chain" of spiritual succession; it embodies the master–disciple bond (1), traces an unbroken lineage back to the Prophet (2), and channels baraka (spiritual power/blessing) down the line (3). All three apply.

subject: History
stem: Which one of the following statements is not correct?
options: Al-Biruni identifies the Sufi doctrine of divine love as self-annihilation with parallel passages from Bhagavad Gita.
According to Al-Biruni, Sufi theories of Soul were similar to those in Patanjali's Yoga Sutra.
The Hatha Yogic treatise Amrita Kunda had lasting impact on Sufism.
Hujwiri's conversation with the Yogis shows that he was impressed with their theory of the division of the human body.
explanation: Al-Biruni's drawing of parallels between Sufi ideas and the Gita/Patanjali, and the influence of the Amrita Kunda on Sufism, are well-documented (a, b, c correct). The claim about Hujwiri being impressed by a yogic "division of the human body" theory is the odd/incorrect one. (This is a specialised question; cross-check with the official key.)

subject: History
stem: What is the name of the literary genre developed by the Khojas who are a branch of the Ismaili sect?
explanation: The Khojas composed Ginans — devotional hymns (in Indian languages) that blended Ismaili ideas with local Indian traditions. Ziyarat (pilgrimage), Raag (musical mode) and Shahada (the Islamic creed) are not this genre.

subject: History
stem: The idea of Farr-i Izadi, on which the Mughal kingship was based, was first developed by which one of the following Sufi saints?
explanation: Farr-i Izadi ("divine effulgence/light of God") — the idea that the king is bathed in divine radiance — derives from the Ishraqi (Illuminationist) philosophy of Shihabuddin Suhrawardi, and Abul Fazl used it to glorify Akbar's kingship.

subject: History
stem: The biography of Shaikh Muinuddin Chishti, authored by Jahanara, is known as
explanation: The Mughal princess Jahanara, a devout follower of the Chishti order, wrote Munis al-Arwah ("The Confidant of Spirits"), a life of Khwaja Muinuddin Chishti. The Fawaid-ul-Fuwad is by Amir Hasan Sijzi.

subject: History
stem: The town of Chisht, from which the Sufi Chishti Silsila derives its name, is located in
explanation: Chisht, near Herat in central Afghanistan, is the place after which the Chishti order is named. It is not in Turkey, Iran or Iraq.

subject: History
stem: Who propounded Kashmir Shaivism?
explanation: Vasugupta (9th century), author of the Shiva Sutras, founded the Kashmir Shaivism (Trika/Pratyabhijna) system. Abhinavagupta was its greatest later exponent, but not the founder.

subject: History
stem: Chandimangala was composed in which one of the following languages during the 16th century CE?
explanation: The Chandimangala (by Mukundaram Chakravarti) belongs to the Bengali Mangalkavya tradition praising local deities — composed in Bengali. Not Sanskrit, Tamil or Oriya.

subject: History
stem: Match List I (Author) with List II (Poetical work): A. Brindavan Das B. Krishnadas Kaviraj C. Mukundaram Chakravarti D. Rameshwar Bhattacharya -- 1. Shivasankirttan 2. Chandimangal 3. Chaitanyacharitamrita 4. Chaitanyamangal.
explanation: Brindavan Das wrote Chaitanyamangal (4); Krishnadas Kaviraj the Chaitanyacharitamrita (3); Mukundaram Chakravarti the Chandimangal (2); Rameshwar Bhattacharya the Shivasankirttan (1). So A-4, B-3, C-2, D-1.

subject: History
stem: Who among the following was the founder of the Virashaiva Movement in Karnataka?
explanation: Basavanna (Basaveshwara), a 12th-century minister-reformer, founded the Virashaiva (Lingayat) movement in Karnataka, rejecting caste and ritual. Sambandar, Appar and Sundarar were earlier Tamil Nayanar saints.

subject: History
stem: Which of the following statements about the Vaishnava saint of Assam, Shankardeva, is/are correct?
subs: 1. His teachings are often known as the Bhagavati dharma.
2. He encouraged satras (monasteries) and namghars (prayer halls) for transmission of spiritual knowledge.
3. His major compositions include the Kirtana-ghosha.
explanation: Shankardeva founded Assam's Vaishnava Eka-Sarana Nama Dharma (Bhagavati/Bhagavata dharma) (1), spread it through satras and namghars (2), and composed the Kirtana-ghosha (3). All three are correct.

subject: History
stem: How many of the following statements about Bhakti poet Namdev is/are correct?
subs: 1. He seems to have played a part in transmitting southern Bhakti to northern India.
2. He was a rigorous monotheist and opposed caste distinctions.
3. He was a devout follower of Kabir.
explanation: Namdev (a Marathi saint) did help carry the Bhakti current northward (1) and was a monotheist opposed to caste (2). Statement 3 is wrong — Namdev predates Kabir and was not his follower (if anything the influence ran the other way). So two are correct.

subject: History
stem: Which of the following statements about Virashaivism is/are correct?
subs: 1. It traces its origin to five great religious teachers -- Renuka, Daruka, Ghantakarna, Dhenukarna and Vishvakarna.
2. The Virashaiva philosophy is called Shaktivishishtadvaita -- the non-duality of God.
3. Ashtavarana are the eight rules of Virashaivism to be observed by the followers.
explanation: Virashaivism claims descent from five legendary teachers (1), its philosophy is Shaktivishishtadvaita (qualified non-dualism through Shakti) (2), and followers must observe the Ashtavarana (eight protective rites, including wearing the ishtalinga) (3). All three are correct.

subject: History
stem: Which one of the following works was written in early eighteenth century by the Bengali poet Rameshwar Bhattacharya?
explanation: Rameshwar Bhattacharya composed the Shivsankirttan (Shivayan), a Bengali poem on the householder life of Shiva. The other titles belong to different poets/genres.

subject: History
stem: The Sahajiya Cult, which can be traced back to 10th and 11th centuries, is usually associated with
explanation: The Sahajiya cult grew out of Tantric Buddhism (Sahajayana), emphasising attaining the "innate/spontaneous" (sahaja) state. It later influenced Vaishnava devotion, but its 10th–11th-century roots are Buddhist.

subject: History
stem: Name the platform used for ritual purposes by the kings of the Vijayanagara Empire.
explanation: The Mahanavami Dibba was a great stepped platform at Hampi from which the king watched and performed rituals during the Mahanavami (Dussehra) festival. The Lotus Mahal and Hazara Rama are other structures; Virupaksha is a temple.

subject: History
stem: The class of Amar Nayakas in Vijayanagara is a reference to which of the following?
explanation: The Amara-Nayakas were military commanders granted territories (amaram) to govern and tax in return for maintaining troops for the king — the backbone of the Vijayanagara political-military system.

subject: History
stem: The Amara-Nayaka system was a major political innovation of which Indian imperial rulers?
explanation: The Amara-Nayaka system — military chiefs holding revenue-bearing territories in exchange for maintaining forces — was the key political innovation of the Vijayanagara Empire (with parallels to the Persian iqta). Not a Chola, Chalukya or Gupta system.

subject: History
stem: Which of the following works was composed by Krishnadevaraya on statecraft in Telugu?
explanation: Krishnadevaraya's Amuktamalyada, in Telugu, combines devotional poetry with shrewd advice on kingship and administration. Jambavati Kalyanam is his Sanskrit play; Rayavachakamu is a later prose chronicle.

subject: History
stem: Which one of the following is the language of the famous political treatise 'Amuktmalyada'?
explanation: The Amuktamalyada was written in Telugu by Krishnadevaraya — reflecting the flowering of Telugu literature under his patronage.

subject: History
stem: Who among the following was not a court poet of king Krishnadevaraya?
explanation: Krishnadevaraya's court had the Ashtadiggajas ("eight elephants") — including Nandi Timmana, Dhurjati and Madayyagari Mallana. Siddheshvara was not among his court poets.

subject: History
stem: With reference to the Amara-Nayaka system, which one of the following statements is not correct?
options: It was a major political innovation of the Vijayanagara Empire
They were military commanders given territories to govern and collect taxes from peasants, craftspersons and traders
They retained part of the revenue for personal use and to maintain stipulated contingent of horses and elephants
They were not allowed to use their share of revenue for the maintenance of temples
explanation: Amara-Nayakas in fact did spend revenue on temples and irrigation works (becoming important patrons) — so statement d is false. The system being a Vijayanagara innovation, the chiefs being revenue-collecting military commanders, and their keeping part of the revenue for contingents are all correct.

subject: History
stem: Consider the following pairs:
subs: 1. Gajapati -- Name of a powerful Dynasty in Orissa
2. Ashvapati -- Deccan Sultans
3. Narapati -- Rayas of Vijayanagara.
explanation: These were medieval royal epithets by military strength: Gajapati ("lord of elephants") = the Orissa dynasty (1); Ashvapati ("lord of horses") = the Deccan/Bahmani Sultans (2); Narapati ("lord of men/infantry") = the Vijayanagara Rayas (3). All three are correct.

subject: History
stem: Domingo Paes, the medieval traveller, has described the city of
explanation: The Portuguese traveller Domingo Paes left a vivid account of the splendour of Vijayanagara (Hampi) at its height under Krishnadevaraya. He did not describe Madurai, Arikamedu or Gingee.

subject: History
stem: Abdur Razzaq, an ambassador of the ruler of Persia, was sent to Calicut in the fifteenth century. He was greatly impressed by
options: Climatic conditions of the coastal town
High quality spices of the place
explanation: Abdur Razzaq (envoy of Shah Rukh) was struck by Calicut's defences, describing its lines/fortifications and its security as a great trading port. Climate, spices and music were not the focus of his praise here.

subject: History
stem: Consider the following statements:
subs: Statement I: The Pahi-kashta peasants were non-resident cultivators cultivating lands on a contractual basis.
Statement II: The Pahi-kashta peasants worked under the temptation of favourable terms of revenue or the compulsion of economic distress.
options: Both the statements are individually true and Statement II is the correct explanation of Statement I
Both the statements are individually true but Statement II is not the correct explanation of Statement I
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
explanation: Pahi-kashta peasants (as opposed to resident khud-kashta) tilled land in villages other than their own, on contract (I). They did so either because they were offered easier revenue terms or because distress drove them off their own land (II) — which is exactly why they cultivated as non-resident contractors. So II explains I.

subject: History
stem: Consider the following statements:
subs: Statement I: The Zamindars were an exploitative class in Mughal India.
Statement II: The Zamindars often received the support of the peasantry in a large number of agrarian uprisings in North India in the seventeenth century.
options: Both the statements are individually true and Statement II is the correct explanation of Statement I
Both the statements are individually true but Statement II is not the correct explanation of Statement I
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
explanation: Both are true, but they pull in different directions — zamindars could be exploitative (I), yet they also led peasants in revolts against the Mughal state (II). The peasant support does not explain their exploitative character; the two are contrasting facts, so II is not the explanation.

subject: History
stem: Which one of the following sources tells us about women protesting against the infidelity of their husbands or the neglect of the wife and children by the male head of the household?
options: Documents belonging to the Village Panchayats of Rajasthan, Gujarat and Maharashtra
explanation: Local village-panchayat records of western India preserve actual complaints by women against neglect or infidelity — a rare window into ordinary women's lives. Al-Biruni's Kitab-ul-Hind, Mandor sculptures and the Ain-i-Akbari do not give this everyday detail.

subject: History
stem: Consider the following statement: "So much is wrung from the peasants, that even dry bread is scarcely left to fill their stomachs." Who among the following European travellers had made the above statement about the condition of peasantry in the Mughal Empire?
explanation: The Dutch factor Francisco Pelsaert (in his Remonstrantie) graphically described the grinding poverty of the Mughal peasantry under heavy revenue demands. Bernier, Tavernier and Manucci made other observations.

subject: History
stem: Which of the following terms were used in the Indo-Persian sources of the Mughal period to denote a peasant?
subs: 1. Raiyat
2. Asami
3. Muzarian
4. Majur.
explanation: Raiyat, Asami and Muzarian were all Indo-Persian words for the cultivating peasant (1, 2, 3). Majur meant a hired labourer/wage-worker, not a peasant. Hence 1, 2 and 3 only.

subject: History
stem: Which one of the following was primarily associated with Dadni system?
explanation: Under the Dadni system, merchants/companies gave weavers advance payments (dadan) against future delivery of cloth — a way to control textile production. It had nothing to do with warfare, officials' pay or land revenue.

subject: History
stem: In medieval India, the process of indigo extraction underwent great improvement as a result of
options: use of fertilizer enhancing the quality of indigo crop
introduction of mechanical crushing tools for indigo stems
use of gypsum and lime mortars in making the inner surfaces of indigo vats water-proof
employment of slave labour in quick desilting of indigo crucibles
explanation: Lining the fermentation/settling vats with gypsum and lime mortar made them watertight, preventing leakage and improving the quality and yield of the indigo dye. Fertilizer, crushing tools and slave labour were not the key improvement.

subject: History
stem: Which of the following statements about Mughal Mansab system are correct?
subs: 1. Zat rank was an indicator of a Mansabdar's position in the imperial hierarchy and the salary of the Mansabdar.
2. Sawar rank indicated the number of horsemen the Mansabdar was required to maintain.
3. In the seventeenth century, Mansabdars holding 1000 or above Sawar rank were designated as nobles (Umara).
explanation: Zat fixed a mansabdar's status and pay (1), Sawar fixed how many cavalry he had to keep (2), and those holding rank of 1000 and above were ranked as nobles/Umara (3). The question treats all three as correct.

subject: History
stem: The important source for Akbar's reign, Tarikh-i-Akbari was written by which one of the following Persian language scholars?
explanation: The Tarikh-i-Akbari (also called Tarikh-i-Arifi) was written by Arif Qandahari. Badauni wrote the Muntakhab-ut-Tawarikh and Nizamuddin Ahmad the Tabaqat-i-Akbari — different histories of the period.

subject: History
stem: The aristocrat Muqarrab Khan was a great favourite of which Mughal Emperor?
explanation: Muqarrab Khan, a noble and skilled physician, was a special favourite of Emperor Jahangir. He was not specially associated with Akbar, Farrukhsiyar or Shah Alam.

subject: History
stem: Akbar issued a Farman in 1598 permitting in the city of Cambay (Khambat), Gujarat, the construction of a
explanation: A surviving 1598 farman of Akbar allowed the Jesuit fathers (Padris) to build a church at Cambay — an example of his policy of religious tolerance (sulh-i-kul). It did not authorise a temple, synagogue or Jain upashraya.

subject: History
stem: Which one of the following is listed in the Ain-i Akbari as one of the Indian sciences prescribed by Akbar for scholars to learn at school?
explanation: Correct answer: Jyotisha (option d). Per the Ain-i Akbari, Akbar prescribed specific Indian sciences for Hindu scholars to learn, and Jyotisha (astronomy/astrology) was explicitly listed among them.

subject: History
stem: [Repeated in paper] Which one of the following is listed in the Ain-i Akbari as one of the Indian sciences prescribed by Akbar for scholars to learn at school?
explanation: Repeated question (same as Q.31): the correct answer is Jyotisha (option d), listed in the Ain-i Akbari among the Indian sciences Akbar prescribed for scholars.

subject: History
stem: 'Man Kautuhal', a work on music, was prepared under the aegis of
explanation: Raja Man Singh Tomar of Gwalior, a great patron of music who helped develop the Dhrupad style, sponsored the musical treatise Man Kautuhal. Tansen, Meera Bai and Amir Khusrau were not its patrons.

subject: History
stem: Which one of the following painters was not associated with Humayun?
explanation: Humayun brought Persian masters such as Mir Sayyid Ali and Abd al-Samad (Dost Musawir's circle) to found the Mughal atelier. Bihzad was the celebrated earlier Herat master (15th century) and was not part of Humayun's painters.

subject: History
stem: Which one of the following scholars of Akbar's court translated Bhaskaracharya's Lilavati into Persian?
explanation: Faizi (Abul Fazl's brother and Akbar's poet laureate) translated the mathematical classic Lilavati (by Bhaskaracharya) into Persian, part of Akbar's translation project. The others did not make this translation.

subject: History
stem: Consider the following statements about Sawai Jai Singh's astronomical work:
subs: 1. He learnt of the accuracy of European observations and obtained de La Hire's tables from which he reproduced a refraction table.
2. His astronomers also developed a telescope of their own to observe the lunar phases of Venus.
explanation: Sawai Jai Singh II of Jaipur was a major patron of astronomy in 18th-century India. He built large masonry observatories called Jantar Mantars at Delhi, Jaipur, Ujjain, Mathura and Varanasi, focusing on accurate naked-eye measurement, and sponsored astronomical tables including the Zij-i-Muhammad Shahi. Statement 2 is incorrect because his astronomers did not develop an independent telescope tradition - his astronomy relied on observational instruments, not telescope-making.

subject: History
stem: How many of the following statements regarding medicine and related practices in medieval India is/are correct?
subs: 1. Indian medicine of the Graeco-Arabic tradition (Tibb-i-Yunani) was almost identical in its practice with contemporary Persian medicine.
2. Harvey's discovery of the circulation of blood was explained to a scholarly noble by European traveller Fran-ois Bernier.
3. The practice of smallpox inoculation was described in contemporary Yunani and Ayurvedic texts.
explanation: Statements 1 and 2 are correct — Tibb-i-Yunani (Greco-Arab medicine) matched contemporary Persian practice, and Bernier did explain Harvey's circulation of the blood to the noble Danishmand Khan. Statement 3 is doubtful — smallpox inoculation was a folk/practical technique, not well-documented in the classical texts. So two are correct.

subject: History
stem: Which one of the following European travellers never returned to Europe and settled down in India?
explanation: Niccolao Manucci, the Italian author of Storia do Mogor, spent the rest of his life in India (dying near Madras/Pondicherry) and never returned to Europe. Barbosa, Tavernier and Bernier returned.

subject: History
stem: According to the French traveller Tavernier, the majority of houses in Varanasi during the 17th century were made of
explanation: Jean-Baptiste Tavernier was a 17th-century French traveller who visited India during the Mughal period; his account gives useful details about cities, trade, diamonds, textiles and urban life. For Varanasi he described houses as built of brick and stone, showing durable urban architecture rather than temporary or mud-based housing. The other options are incorrect because they pair brick with mud, wood or lime in ways that do not match Tavernier's description.

subject: History
stem: Which European traveller had observed, "A Hindu woman can go anywhere alone, even in the most crowded places, and she need never fear the impertinent looks and jokes of idle loungers"?
explanation: The French missionary Abbe J. A. Dubois, a close observer of South Indian society, made this remark on the safety of Hindu women in public. Bernier, Tavernier and Thomas Roe wrote about other matters.

subject: History
stem: Fran-ois Bernier was physician to
explanation: The French doctor-traveller Bernier served as physician to Dara Shikoh (and later attached himself to the noble Danishmand Khan), giving him an insider's view of the Mughal court and the war of succession.

subject: History
stem: Al-Biruni's Kitab-ul-Hind was written in which language?
explanation: Al-Biruni wrote his great study of India, the Kitab-ul-Hind (Tahqiq-i-Hind), in Arabic, the scholarly language of his world. (Urdu did not yet exist as a literary language.)

subject: History
stem: Which one of the following statements about the Sayyid brothers during the period of Later Mughals is not true?
options: They brought Jahandar Shah to power.
They followed a tolerant religious policy.
They reached an agreement with King Shahu.
explanation: The Sayyid brothers ("king-makers") raised Farrukhsiyar to the throne (defeating Jahandar Shah) — they did not bring Jahandar Shah to power, so statement a is false. They did dominate administration, abolish the jizya (tolerant policy), and make terms with the Maratha king Shahu.

subject: History
stem: Who was the first Nawab Wazir of Awadh in the 18th century?
explanation: Saadat Khan (Burhan-ul-Mulk) founded the autonomous Awadh dynasty in 1722 — the first Nawab. (Safdarjung later also held the imperial Wazir title, but the founder of the line was Saadat Khan.)

subject: History
stem: Which one of the following Mughal emperors was a follower of the Naqshbandiyya leader, Khwaja Ubaydullah Ahrar?
explanation: Babur, in his memoirs, expresses deep devotion to the Naqshbandi Sufi Khwaja Ubaydullah Ahrar of Samarqand (he even versified one of Ahrar's tracts). The later emperors are not specifically identified as his disciples.

subject: History
stem: The term Upari refers to which one of the following?
options: A category of proprietary tenure under the Mughal rule
A category of tenancy tenure under the Maratha rule
A soldier in the Maratha army
A village headman in the Mughal period
explanation: Under Maratha land revenue, an Upari was a temporary, non-hereditary tenant cultivator (as opposed to the hereditary Mirasdar). It was not a Mughal tenure, a soldier or a headman.

subject: History
stem: Which Maratha statesman signed the Treaty of Bassein on December 31, 1802?
